cataloging_subject.label: The Seven Sages of Rome (Northern Version)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the anonymous Northern Middle English verse redaction of The Seven Sages of Rome, represented by Text C in British Library Cotton MS Galba E IX and the incomplete Text R in Bodleian Library MS Rawl. poet. 175. It excludes the wider multilingual Seven Sages / Book of Sindbad tradition, the broader Middle English Version A and Y Group, other Middle English redactions, the complete manuscript miscellanies, Campbell's 1907 edition and apparatus, the U-M encoding, the repository XML, and digital facsimiles as distinct layers.

catalog_summary.title: The Seven Sages of Rome (Northern Version)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Composed by about 1350, perhaps in the first half of the fourteenth century; attested in a mid- to late-fourteenth-century witness; portions first printed in 1810 and the complete Northern text in 1907
catalog_summary.region: Middle English work with a Northern English dialect association; exact place of composition is unknown; frame narrative set in Rome

titles.value: The Seven Sages of Rome (Northern Version)

assertion.notes: The parenthetical qualifier distinguishes this redaction from the Midland and Southern versions; it is not a medieval rubric.

titles.value: Here Bigyns þe Proces of þe Seuyn Sages
notes: The XML records that R has spelling variants in the heading.

titles.value: Lordynges þat here likes to dwell

notes: Campbell's final verse line is followed by the prose closer AMEN.

agents.name: Killis Campbell
notes: Killis Campbell (1872–1937) was editor and collator of the immediate 1907 source edition, not the medieval author.

assertion.notes: The work is an anonymous Northern redaction within the Middle English Version A / Y tradition. A later attribution to Richard Rolle is unsupported.

notes: Campbell based the text on Cotton Galba E IX, collated Rawlinson poet. 175, and cited Weber as W; his supplied punctuation, paragraphing, capitalization, word division, expansions, and emendations belong to the edition layer.

date_statements.display: The Northern redaction existed by about 1350; Campbell tentatively proposed the first quarter of the fourteenth century for the lost common exemplar behind C and R

assertion.notes: The terminus is inferred from the earliest surviving witness. Campbell's narrower proposal concerns a reconstructed exemplar and is not normalized as a secure composition interval.

date_statements.display: Bodleian Library MS Rawl. poet. 175, the earlier incomplete witness R, is dated to the second half of the fourteenth century (1350–1400)
Henry Weber printed a composite Northern/Southern text in 1810, including the Cotton opening and ending

scope.description: Northern Text C opening and ending within Weber's composite Proces of the Seuyn Sages

date_statements.display: Campbell published the complete Northern Text C with R variants in 1907

places.label: Northern England
notes: This deliberately broad label avoids treating Campbell's tentative York-neighborhood suggestion as established.

assertion.notes: Northern is secure as the redactional and linguistic classification; exact composition at York is not established.

notes: Rome is the frame narrative's court setting; embedded stories have varied or generic settings.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English and Latin occur in Campbell's editorial apparatus and labels, not as intrinsic languages of the medieval work.

form_statements.description: A Middle English verse romance in predominantly octosyllabic rhyming couplets, lineated by Campbell to 4,328 lines.

subject_statements.label: False accusation, delayed execution, and rash judgment

assertion.notes: The classification describes a sustained rhetorical pattern while allowing for the tales' competing speakers and unreliable counsel.

subject_statements.label: Education, astrological foreknowledge, and enforced silence

summaries.value: A Roman emperor sends his son to seven sages for education. On the prince's return, astrological foreknowledge requires seven days of silence; his stepmother propositions him and, when refused, falsely accuses him. The emperor repeatedly orders the prince's execution, while the Empress and sages alternate cautionary tales until the prince can speak, expose the plot, and tell his own final tale. The Northern redaction is distinguished by its C/R textual tradition and includes a distinctive witchcraft episode.

After a 592-line opening frame, the XML alternates fifteen prologues and fifteen story divisions: Arbor, Canis, Aper, Medicus, Gaza, Puteus, Senescalcus, Tentamina, Virgilius, Avis, Sapientes, Vidua, Roma, Inclusa, and Vaticinium, with the frame conclusion in the final division.

Campbell's printed story names, folio markers, lineation, punctuation, paragraphing, and editorial italics are navigational or editorial features, not all recoverable authorial divisions. The XML's total of 4,328 L elements is accidentally equal to Campbell's lineation: only 4,326 are base-text lines, while two are Rawlinson-only variant lines nested in a footnote and two Campbell/Cotton lines are absent.

extent.description: Campbell's complete base text is lineated 1–4,328. The XML has 31 direct work LG elements containing 4,326 base-text L elements: 592 opening-frame lines and 3,734 lines across thirty alternating prologue/story divisions. A thirty-second LG, nested in a footnote after Campbell line 2364, contains two Rawlinson-only added lines. Thus count(//L)=4,328 does not mean the complete 4,328-line base text is present: Campbell/Cotton lines 1832 and 3533 are omitted. The XML also has 30 DIV2, 32 HEAD, 149 PB, 958 MILESTONE, and 3,509 NOTE elements. These are representation metrics, not a claim that every editorial division is authorial.

content_parts.label: Opening frame narrative
content_parts.summary: The emperor, prince, seven sages, education, remarriage, astrological danger, return to court, accusation, and first threatened execution through the demand for the first delaying tale.

content_parts.label: Fifteen prologue-and-story pairs and frame conclusion
content_parts.summary: Fifteen alternating speeches and embedded stories, ending with the prince's Vaticinium story and the exposure, judgment, and execution of the Empress.
notes: The encoded metric is two lines short of Campbell's 3,736-line scholarly range because base-text lines 1832 and 3533 are absent; the two nested R-only lines are apparatus and are not counted here.

related_works.title: Y Group of Middle English Version A
notes: Current specialist work places the Northern and Southern redactions within the Y Group, descended from a lost source y; the reconstructed source is not treated as an extant lineage entity.

related_works.title: Middle English Version A of The Seven Sages of Rome
notes: The broader Middle English Version A is dated approximately 1275–1320; that range is not transferred to the Northern redaction.

related_works.title: The Seven Sages of Rome (Midland Version)
notes: The Midland Version is a separate Middle English Version A redaction, not a witness of the Northern work.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00033.xml.
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for identifiers, Middle English language, Campbell title page, rubric, incipit, narrative, story divisions, apparatus, line and page milestones, explicit, abbreviations, and documented 2016 review.

evidence.citation: Structural and milestone inspection of CME00033.xml using the repository inspection script plus direct XML-path counts and comparison with Campbell's lineation.
evidence.locator: DIV1, DIV2, HEAD, LG, L, PB, MILESTONE, NOTE; direct-work versus NOTE-nested LG/L; line milestones around 1832 and 3533
evidence.summary: Finds 4 DIV1, 30 DIV2, 32 HEAD, 32 LG, 4,328 L, 149 PB, 958 MILESTONE, and 3,509 NOTE elements. Only 31 LG and 4,326 L belong to the base text; one NOTE-nested LG holds two R-only lines. Comparison with Campbell shows omitted base lines 1832 and 3533, with milestone offsets increasing after each omission.

evidence.citation: Killis Campbell, ed., The Seven Sages of Rome (Boston: Ginn & Company, 1907), complete scan.
evidence.locator: preface pp. v–viii; introduction pp. ix–cxiv; edited text pp. 1–145; notes and glossary pp. 146–217
evidence.summary: Immediate-edition evidence for title, authorship caution, C and R descriptions, C-based editorial method, C/R relationship through lost cr, lineation, verse and dialect, textual structure, complete work, apparatus, and publication history.

evidence.citation: Library of Congress, The seven sages of Rome, LCCN 07005077.
evidence.summary: Authoritative bibliographic evidence for Campbell's editorship, 1907 Ginn imprint, Albion Series, extent cxiv plus 217 pages, LCCN 07005077, OCLC 351974, and linked digital copy.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Cotton MS Galba E IX, ff. 3–114, record 041-001607569.
evidence.locator: contents, including The Seuyn Sages of Rome at ff. 25v–48v; date; language; access
evidence.summary: Official holding evidence for shelfmark, first-quarter-fifteenth-century date, Middle English content, work folios 25v–48v within ff. 3–114, and physical consultation route.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Rawl. poet. 175, Summary Catalogue 14667.
evidence.summary: Official holding evidence for the parchment English codex, second-half-fourteenth-century date, Rawlinson bequest, and shelfmark; its broad contents description does not isolate the Seven Sages folios.

evidence.citation: The Seven Sages of Rome Database, London, British Library, Cotton Galba E IX.
evidence.locator: Text C; Northern Version; Y Group; Middle English Version A; ff. 25v–48v; date and incipit
evidence.summary: Current specialist evidence for the complete C witness, its textual hierarchy, work folios, Northern language, 1400–1425 range, and Campbell edition; the project identifies itself as work in progress.

evidence.citation: The Seven Sages of Rome Database, Oxford, Bodleian Library, Rawlinson poet. 175.
evidence.locator: Eng7; Text R; Northern Version; Y Group; ff. 109r–131v; production and physical description
evidence.summary: Current specialist evidence for the incomplete R witness, work folios, textual hierarchy, parchment codex, 1350–1400 production, dimensions, and verse status.

evidence.citation: The Seven Sages of Rome Database, Northern English / Northern Version.
evidence.summary: Current specialist classification evidence connecting Texts C and R as the Northern redaction within the Y Group and Middle English Version A.

evidence.citation: The Seven Sages of Rome Database, Middle English Version A and Y Group.
evidence.summary: Current specialist evidence for the broader Middle English Version A's adaptation from Old French A, approximate 1275–1320 date, three-redaction organization, the Y Group's lost common source, and the database's work-in-progress status.

evidence.citation: Henry Weber, ed., Metrical Romances of the Thirteenth, Fourteenth, and Fifteenth Centuries, vol. III (Edinburgh, 1810), complete scan.
evidence.locator: The Proces of the Seuyn Sages, pp. 1–153; Various Readings
evidence.summary: Evidence that Weber's 1810 printed text is composite: Auchinleck supplies the central surviving fragment, while Cotton supplies the opening and ending; Campbell cites Weber's text as W.

evidence.citation: Hannah Zeldenrust, Hating Women in the Middle English Seven Sages of Rome, Review of English Studies 77, no. 328 (2026): 1–17.
evidence.summary: Recent peer-reviewed evidence for the Middle English witness grouping, romance and frame-tale interpretation, antifeminist discourse and competing narrators, a likely lost Middle English translation around 1300, and a distinctive Northern witch episode.

open_questions.question: When and where was the Northern redaction composed, and how exactly does it relate to the reconstructed lost C/R exemplar cr and the broader Y ancestor?
next_steps: Consult post-Campbell stemmatic and dialect studies before narrowing the date below the terminus supplied by R.
Treat Campbell's first-quarter-fourteenth-century and York-neighborhood suggestions as tentative.
Do not create extant-source lineage entities for reconstructed cr or y.

open_questions.question: Why does the U-M / repository encoding omit Campbell/Cotton base-text lines 1832 and 3533 while including two R-only lines as nested L elements in an apparatus note?
next_steps: Collate the source XML systematically against Campbell's pp. 1–145 and the U-M displayed text.
Ask U-M whether the omissions are known keying defects or reflect an earlier source file.
Keep representation metrics separated from Campbell's 4,328-line scholarly extent.

open_questions.question: What initial keying method, correction history, and source-file terms apply to the U-M CME00033 encoding before its documented 2016 review?
next_steps: Request item-level production metadata and any source package from U-M.
Keep the 2016 TEMPHEAD date scoped to review rather than creation.
Do not infer OCR versus manual transcription from the current XML alone.

notes: Work, witnesses, scholarly editions, electronic encoding, repository artifact, facsimiles, and rights are modeled as separate layers.
C and R are independent descendants of a reconstructed lost common exemplar in Campbell's analysis; neither is asserted to have been copied from the other.
The Auchinleck manuscript is a parallel Southern/Y witness used by Weber's composite edition, not a direct witness of the Northern redaction.
